1: Eliphaz’s third speech marks a decisive escalation in the friends’ strategy. In the previous two speeches (Job 4–5; 15) Eliphaz argued from general principle; here he shifts to specific accusation (yakah), cataloguing concrete social crimes as the evidence of Job’s guilt (vv6–9). This shift from deductive argument to evidentiary accusation mirrors the procedure of the ancient Near Eastern legal tribunal: when abstract theology fails to produce a confession, the accuser pivots to specific charges. In Mesopotamian legal practice, the accuser in a formal diinu (case) was required to state specific wrongs; Eliphaz is constructing a forensic brief.
2: The rhetorical question — “Can a man be profitable to God?” — establishes Eliphaz’s foundational theological premise: God is entirely self-sufficient and therefore cannot be motivated by personal interest in human prosperity or suffering. This doctrine of divine aseity (self-existence without need) is found across ancient wisdom traditions. The Instruction of Amenemope emphasizes that the gods have no material need of human offerings; Mesopotamian theology likewise debated whether the gods required humans for their own sustenance (cf. the Atrahasis creation-myth: humanity was created to relieve the lesser gods of labor, implying divine need). Eliphaz argues from the opposite premise: precisely because God needs nothing from Job, God’s rebuke cannot be motivated by self-interest, and therefore must be justice.
3: Eliphaz extends the rhetorical argument: it is not advantage to God if Job is righteous, nor harm if he is wicked. This is the “no-advantage” premise that grounds all the friends’ theology. In Egyptian wisdom, Ma’at (cosmic order/truth/justice) was a principle independent of divine interest — the gods themselves maintained it not for personal gain but because order was the structure of reality. Eliphaz is operating within a similar framework: the divine order of retributive justice functions independently of divine self-interest, making it all the more reliable as a moral principle.
4: Eliphaz’s question — “Is it because of your fear of him that he reproves you?” — creates the sarcastic implication: clearly God is not reproving Job because Job is too pious. The word yir’ah (fear) here deliberately echoes the four-descriptor portrait of 1:1 and Job 1:8 (yere ’elohim, fearing God). Eliphaz inverts this portrait: Job’s suffering proves that his yir’ah is not the real cause of God’s action. This inversion is deeply ironic for the reader who knows from the prologue that Job’s yir’ah is precisely what initiated the entire test.
5: The rhetorical build culminates in Eliphaz’s direct accusation: Job’s wickedness is great (rabbah) and his iniquities have no end (’en qets). The concluding question “Is not your wickedness great?” follows the ma (hal´) interrogative pattern used in legal accusation throughout the Hebrew Bible. In ancient Near Eastern legal documents, the formal accusation (tuhu in Akkadian) often preceded the specific evidence catalog; Eliphaz reverses this by first establishing the conclusion (v5) and then providing the evidence (vv6–9). This is rhetorically aggressive: verdict first, then alleged facts.
6: The specific crime of “taking pledges (haval) from your brothers for nothing” reflects the well-documented ancient Near Eastern practice of accepting a debtor’s cloak or tool as security for a loan. Mosaic Law explicitly regulated this: Exodus 22:26–27 required that a man’s outer garment taken as a pledge be returned before nightfall because it was his only covering. Deuteronomy 24:12–13 expanded this protection. The Hammurabi Code (ca. 1754 BCE, Laws 66–67) regulated pledge-taking from debtors. Eliphaz accuses Job of taking pledges “for nothing” (hinnam) — without legitimate cause — the same word used in Job 1:9 (the adversary’s “does Job fear God for nothing?”) and Job 2:3 (YHWH’s admission that Job was ruined “for nothing”). The ironic echo of hinnam connects Eliphaz’s false accusation to the book’s actual theological crux.
7: Withholding water from the weary and bread from the hungry are the quintessential violations of ANE hospitality ethics. The duty to provide water and food to travelers and the needy was not merely a moral custom but a legal obligation in many ancient Near Eastern societies. The Egyptian negative confession in the Book of the Dead (Spell 125) includes among the declarations of innocence: “I have not been deaf to words of truth; I have not stopped my ears against words of right and wrong; I have not taken away the food-offerings in the temples; I have not held back the bread of the gods.” The categories Eliphaz uses (vv6–9) correspond almost exactly to the Egyptian negative-confession inventory of social crimes that a righteous person must deny — Eliphaz is constructing what amounts to a reverse negative-confession for Job.
8: The contrast between the powerful man (’ish zero’a, man of arm/power) who possesses the land and the honored man who dwells in it versus the fate of the widow/orphan reflects the standard legal distinction in ancient Near Eastern society between those with social power and those without legal standing. In ANE legal systems, widows (almanot) and orphans (yetomim) had no male advocate to represent them in legal proceedings. The king was in theory their protector (Psalms 68:5: “father to the fatherless, protector of widows, is God in his holy habitation”; 1 Kings 8:45). Eliphaz accuses Job of exploiting precisely this structural vulnerability.
9: The specific accusations against Job concerning widows sent away empty-handed and orphans whose arms were crushed follow the standard ANE social-justice indictment pattern. The identical categories appear in prophetic indictments: Isaiah 1:17 (“seek justice, correct oppression, bring justice to the fatherless, plead the widow’s cause”); Isaiah 10:2 (the wicked who “turn aside the needy from justice and rob the poor of my people of their right, that widows may be their spoil, and that they may make the fatherless their prey”); Ezekiel 22:7 (“father and mother are treated with contempt; the sojourner suffers extortion; the fatherless and widow are wronged”). This catalog is the standard accusation genre; Eliphaz has applied it to Job with no evidence because his theology demands that Job must have committed such crimes.
10: The snares and sudden terror that surround Job are now reinterpreted by Eliphaz as logical consequences of his supposed wickedness. The word pah (snare/trap) was used in wisdom literature for the traps that the wicked set for others (Proverbs 22:5; Psalms 91:3) and that eventually trap the wicked themselves. Eliphaz’s logic is tight within the retributive framework: the sufferings Job experiences are precisely the categories of harm that the socially oppressive person brings on others, now returned to the oppressor.
11: The darkness and flood imagery in v10–11 uses the standard ANE motifs of cosmic chaos overtaking the wicked: “darkness so you cannot see” and “a flood of water covers you” are the conditions of the pre-creation chaos state (Genesis 1:2: darkness over the deep; waters covering everything). The return of the wicked to the chaos-state is a common theme in both Mesopotamian theological poetry (the punishment of the wicked by chaos-reversal) and in biblical poetry (Psalms 18:4–5: the cords of Sheol and torrents of destruction). For Eliphaz, Job’s present darkness and flood-like suffering are the natural consequences of his social wickedness.
12: Eliphaz accuses Job of thinking God is too distant to observe his wickedness: “Is not God high in the heavens? See the highest stars, how lofty they are!” (v12). This implicitly charges Job with a practical form of divine-absence theology: acting as though God’s exaltation means his distance from human affairs. The Babylonian wisdom text Ludlul bel nemeqi contains a parallel concern: “I wish I knew that these things were pleasing to one’s god. What is proper to oneself is an offence to one’s god.” In both Mesopotamian theology and Eliphaz’s argument, the question of whether the divine is truly present to and concerned with human moral conduct is a central theological challenge.
13: “What does God know?” — Eliphaz attributes this implicit skepticism to Job, positioning Job as holding a theology of divine ignorance (v13) or inaccessibility (v14: the thick clouds are his covering, he cannot see). This philosophical position — that the gods are too exalted to be aware of or concerned with human particulars — was a recognized theological position in the ancient world. Epicurus later made the divine indifference to human affairs a formal philosophical doctrine. The OT consistently argued against this: Psalms 11:4 (“the LORD is in his holy temple; the LORD’s throne is in heaven; his eyes see, his eyelids test the children of man”); Proverbs 15:3 (“the eyes of YHWH are in every place, keeping watch on the evil and the good”).
14: The “thick clouds” (avei shehaqim) as a covering for God repeats the theophany language familiar from Sinai (Exodus 19:9: “I am coming to you in a thick cloud”; Psalms 18:11–12: “He made darkness his covering, his canopy around him, thick clouds dark with water”). Eliphaz turns this theophanic imagery against Job: the clouds that manifest divine presence in theophany become, in Eliphaz’s accusation, the excuse Job allegedly uses for thinking God cannot observe him. The irony is that the same clouds that hide God from sinners disclose God to the righteous; Eliphaz is accusing Job of the sinner’s misreading of divine hiddenness.
15: Eliphaz’s rhetorical question — “Will you keep to the old way that wicked men have trod?” — invokes the ancient precedent argument: the fate of the primordial wicked (me’olam, from of old) serves as warning for the present. The “old way” was a recognized rhetorical category in ancient wisdom: appeals to the precedent of the ancestors (“the ancient wisdom”, the rishon’im) authenticated moral and legal claims. Bildad appealed to it in Job 8:8 (“ask the former age”); now Eliphaz uses the same appeal but in the negative sense: the ancient wicked are the warning precedent.
16: The ancient wicked who were “snatched away before their time, their foundation poured out as a stream” likely refers to the flood generation (Gen 6–8) and/or the primordial wicked of antediluvian tradition. In the ANE, the flood was the paradigmatic act of divine judgment against a morally corrupt generation; Mesopotamian flood accounts (Atrahasis, Utnapishtim episode in Gilgamesh) similarly frame the flood as divine judgment against human wickedness. The image of a foundation poured out like a river (yitstaq nahar yesodham) suggests the collapse of a society as its moral foundation liquefies.
17: The accusation that the wicked said to God “Depart from us!” and “What can the Almighty do to us?” echoes Psalms 10:4 (“In the pride of his face the wicked does not seek him; all his thoughts are, There is no God”) and Psalms 14:1 (“the fool says in his heart, There is no God”). The statement is not intellectual atheism but practical rejection of divine moral authority. By implying that Job holds this attitude, Eliphaz places him in the category of the primordial wicked — the most severe accusation possible within the wisdom tradition’s moral taxonomy.
18: “Yet he filled their houses with good things” — Eliphaz acknowledges the theological problem his own argument creates: the wicked prospered materially before their destruction. This is precisely the problem that the Psalms of Asaph (Ps 73) and the Babylonian Theodicy wrestle with. Eliphaz’s solution is the temporal delay of judgment: God filled their houses first, then destroyed them. The “counsel of the wicked is far from me” disclaimer at the end of v18 has puzzled interpreters — in context, it seems to be Eliphaz disclaiming the wicked’s theology, but its exact syntactical relationship to the preceding material is debated.
19: The righteous seeing the judgment of the wicked and rejoicing, the innocent mocking the wicked, is a sentiment that appears in the Psalter’s treatment of divine judgment (Psalms 52:6: “the righteous shall see and fear, and shall laugh at him”; Psalms 58:10–11). In the ancient world, the public display of divine justice — the visible punishment of the wicked — served a social function of vindicating the righteous and confirming the moral order. The judicial execution of criminals was typically public in ancient Near Eastern societies for precisely this reason: moral deterrence and vindication of the community’s moral standards.
20: “Our enemy has been cut down, and what they left the fire has consumed” — the complete destruction formula (the person AND their possessions consumed) reflects the ANE category of herem or total destruction. In Israelite law, the devoted/cursed thing (herem) was totally destroyed (Joshua 7:24–26 the destruction of Achan and all his property). In Mesopotamian conquest records, the Assyrian annals describe the total destruction of enemy cities: their populations killed, their structures burned, their land plowed and salted. The righteous here are imagined as witnesses to this total elimination of the wicked and their legacy.
21: Eliphaz’s penitential call — “Agree with God and be at peace; thereby good will come to you” — initiates a section that follows the structure of the ancient Near Eastern penitential prayer genre. Mesopotamian penitential prayers (the ershahunga genre, “prayers to appease the heart of a god”) follow a standard pattern: acknowledgment of divine power, confession of sin, prayer for mercy, and the expectation of restoration. Eliphaz offers Job the same template: submit (saken, agree/acquiesce), receive instruction (Torah), repent, and restoration will follow. The theology is internally coherent; the problem is that Job is innocent.
22: The call to “receive instruction from his mouth and place his words in your heart” uses Torah-instruction vocabulary. The word torah in its pre-Pentateuchal sense means “instruction, teaching” — the guidance given by a parent, sage, or teacher (Proverbs 1:8: “hear, my son, your father’s instruction (musar), and forsake not your mother’s teaching (torah)”). “Placing words in the heart” is the standard idiom for internalized wisdom (Deuteronomy 6:6: “these words that I command you today shall be on your heart”; Jeremiah 31:33: the new covenant where God writes his law on the heart). Eliphaz calls Job to the fundamental wisdom posture of receptive listening.
23: “If you return to the Almighty you will be built up” — the Hebrew tibaneh (you will be built) uses the architectural/family-building metaphor for restoration and prosperity. The verb banah (to build) is used in the sense of establishing/building up a household, especially in the idiom “he will build you a house” (2 Samuel 7:11: YHWH will build David a dynasty). The call to “remove injustice far from your tents” uses tent-dwelling imagery appropriate to the book’s patriarchal setting and echoes Job’s own use of tent imagery in later chapters.
24: “Lay gold in the dust and Ophir gold among the stones of the brook” — Eliphaz calls Job to devalue wealth as the condition of spiritual restoration. Ophir (’Ofir) was the legendary gold-producing region of the ancient world; 1 Kgs 9:28 describes Solomon’s trading ships returning from Ophir with 420 talents of gold (cf. 1 Kings 10:11; Job 22:48; 2 Chronicles 8:18). The exact location of Ophir is debated (proposals include southern Arabia, East Africa/Punt, and the Indian subcontinent), but its name was synonymous with the finest gold throughout the ancient world. The call to treat Ophir gold as river-gravel is the most extreme ancient expression of material detachment.
25: The Shaddai (the Almighty) as “your gold and silver treasure” (“let the Almighty be your gold and your precious silver”) uses the substitution formula of wisdom theology: wisdom/God is more valuable than gold (cf. Proverbs 3:14: wisdom’s gain is better than silver; Proverbs 8:19: fruit of wisdom is better than gold; Psalms 19:10: God’s law is more desirable than gold, even much fine gold). This substitution of God for precious metal is the climax of Eliphaz’s investment argument: repudiate material wealth, invest in divine relationship, receive greater returns.
26: The description of delighting in Shaddai and lifting the face to God uses the standard vocabulary of restored divine-human relationship. “Lifting the face” (nasa panim) is the idiom for the confident person who does not need to bow in shame (cf. Ezra 9:6: “I am ashamed to lift my face to you” in the shame of confessed sin; the Aaronic blessing in Numbers 6:26: “the LORD lift up his countenance upon you” = divine favor). Eliphaz offers Job the prospect of restored bilateral favor: Job lifts his face in confidence; God lifts his face in blessing.
27: The prayer-and-hearing formula — “you will make your prayer to him and he will hear you” — is the core of Eliphaz’s promised restoration. The penitential Psalms operate on this exact model: confession + petition = divine hearing + restoration (Psalms 32:5–7; Psalms 51:7–12). The Mesopotamian penitential ershahunga prayers follow the identical logic: the sufferer acknowledges his unknown sin, appeals to the god’s mercy, and expects the god’s appeasement and restoration of favor. Eliphaz is offering Job access to a recognized, tested template of spiritual restoration. The tragedy is that Job’s situation is not the one this template was designed for.
28: The fulfilling of vows when restoration comes follows the standard ancient Near Eastern vow-structure: a petition made under duress included a conditional vow (if you deliver me, I will offer X). When the prayer was answered, the vow was fulfilled publicly (Psalms 22:25–26: “my vows I will perform before those who fear him... they shall eat and be satisfied; those who seek him shall praise the LORD”; Psalms 116:14, 18: “I will pay my vows to the LORD”). Eliphaz’s promise that Job will pay his vows includes the implicit promise that Job’s restoration will be public enough to warrant public vow-fulfillment.
29: The cosmic scope of the restoration promise — “you will decree a thing and it will be established for you; light will shine on your ways” — is the most extraordinary element of Eliphaz’s offer. The promise that a human’s decree will be established (vataqam) echoes the vocabulary used for divine decrees in the ANE (the Mesopotamian simtum, divine decree that determines fate). To offer a human being quasi-divine decree-power upon restoration is the highest theological promise the wisdom tradition could make. “Light will shine on your ways” reverses Job’s self-description of walking in darkness (cf. Job 29:3: “by his light I walked through darkness”).
30: The social implications of restored divine favor — Job will be able to intercede for others and they will be delivered — reflects the ancient understanding that spiritual greatness created intercessory power. Abraham interceded for Abimelech (Genesis 20:17); Moses interceded for Israel (Exodus 32:11–14; Numbers 14:13–20); Samuel declared that to cease interceding would be sin (1 Samuel 12:23). The phrase “the innocent man will be delivered” (nimlat nekiy-kappayim) uses the kappayim (palms-clean) phrase for moral innocence — the same term used in Psalms 24:4 for those who may ascend the holy mountain. Eliphaz ends with an offer of restored mediatorial dignity: Job as intercessor for others. The supreme irony is that in the epilogue (Job 42:7–8), this is precisely what God assigns Job to do.
